meta.note: Greece + Italy + Poland sweep, published books. Ban-adds via existing_book_id: Lady Chatterley's Lover (IT), And Tango Makes Three (IT), Animal Farm (PL), 1984 (PL), The Gulag Archipelago (PL). Inny świat = A World Apart (one entry, English title). Held: Greece's Pericles' Funeral Oration (an excerpt, not a book) and the Aristophanes 'Birds' staging (performance ban). Ancient classics banned from teaching under Metaxas use null pub year. Ferdydurke/Katyn ban years set to avoid ban<publication.

entries.title: The High Mountains
entries.description_ban: Children's school-reader (Ta Psilá Vouná) withdrawn under the Metaxas '4th of August' regime as part of its purge of liberal school texts.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Metaxas-era ban; documented by the Metaxas Project (medium confidence).

entries.title: Life in the Tomb
entries.description_ban: Anti-war WWI novel (I Zoí en Táfo) named among works burned/banned under the Metaxas regime.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Metaxas-era ban; documented by the Metaxas Project (medium confidence).

entries.title: Antigone
entries.description_ban: The Metaxas regime banned the teaching of Sophocles' Antigone in Greek schools as politically subversive.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Metaxas school ban; documented by the Metaxas Project (medium confidence).

entries.title: The Republic
entries.description_ban: Plato's Republic was among the classical texts whose teaching in Greek schools was banned by the Metaxas regime.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Metaxas school ban; documented by the Metaxas Project (medium confidence).

entries.title: M to the Power of N
entries.description_ban: A Thessaloniki court enjoined distribution of the novel in northern Greece on blasphemy grounds (depicting sexual desires of Jesus); the author and publisher were indicted and copies were publicly burned by fundamentalists.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific court injunction on blasphemy grounds; documented by IFEX and Freedom House.

entries.title: Tecnica del colpo di Stato
entries.description_ban: Mussolini blocked the Italian publication of this study of coups (which criticised Hitler); Malaparte was expelled from the Fascist party and confined to Lipari. Not published in Italy until 1948.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Fascist ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: La mascherata
entries.description_ban: Initially cleared, then seized at its second edition by Fascist censorship as a veiled satire of Mussolini and dictatorship; reprinting was forbidden.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Fascist ban; documented via Italian Wikipedia.

entries.title: Le ambizioni sbagliate
entries.description_ban: Withdrawn from bookshops after the 1938 racial laws (Moravia was of partly Jewish descent); reprints were forbidden and remaining stock sold off only after protests.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific racial-law withdrawal; documented in Modern Italy (Cambridge).

entries.title: Tre operai
entries.description_ban: Mussolini judged it a 'communist novel'; newspapers were ordered to ignore it and free circulation of the book was prohibited, and Bernari's passport withdrawn.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Fascist ban; documented by ANPI.

entries.title: Il garofano rosso
entries.description_ban: Serialised in Solaria 1933–34; an installment's issue was seized and later installments censored on moral grounds; Mondadori's 1938 book submission was rejected, so it appeared only in 1948.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Fascist-era censorship; documented via Italian Wikipedia.

entries.title: Lady Chatterley's Lover
entries.description_ban: Rome police ordered all copies seized, and in April 1947 PM De Gasperi ordered Mondadori's Italian-edition stock withdrawn; acquitted of obscenity at a 1959 trial. Adds an Italy ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds an Italy ban; documented via Italian Wikipedia.

entries.title: Porci con le ali
entries.description_ban: In December 1976 a prosecutor ordered the bestselling novel seized for explicit sexual content; copies returned to shops by late January 1977 after a parliamentary question.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific prosecutorial seizure; documented via Italian Wikipedia.

entries.title: Altri libertini
entries.description_ban: Seized in 1980 on a prosecutor's order for blasphemy/obscenity and 'outrage to public morals'; Tondelli was tried in 1981 and fully acquitted.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific prosecutorial seizure; documented in critical literature.

entries.title: Jean a deux mamans
entries.description_ban: Named in Venice Mayor Brugnaro's June 2015 circular among same-sex-family ('gender') titles to be removed from municipal pre-schools.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific municipal removal; documented by Topipittori.

entries.title: And Tango Makes Three
entries.description_ban: One of 49 titles withdrawn from Venice municipal nursery/pre-schools by Mayor Brugnaro in June 2015 (a same-sex-parenting penguin story). Adds an Italy restriction to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds an Italy restriction; documented via Italian Wikipedia.
entries.source_name: Wikipedia (it) — E con Tango siamo in tre

entries.title: A World Apart
entries.description_ban: Herling-Grudziński's Gulag memoir (Inny świat) was banned in communist Poland until 1988; it circulated only via émigré (London) and underground editions.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Ferdydurke
entries.description_ban: Banned in turn by the Nazis, the Stalinists and the PRL regime; briefly reissued in the 1956–57 thaw then blacklisted again until 1989–90.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: Trans-Atlantyk
entries.description_ban: Officially issued in Poland only during the brief 1957 liberalisation, then banned again with all Gombrowicz's work for the rest of the communist period.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: The Issa Valley
entries.description_ban: Listed on the communist censorship index; Miłosz's works were proscribed after his 1951 defection. (Distinct from his titles already held.)
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL ban; documented by the University of Kansas 'Banned Books in Communist Poland' guide.
entries.source_name: University of Kansas — Banned Books in Communist Poland

entries.title: Main Currents of Marxism
entries.description_ban: Could not be published in the PRL; first issued in Paris, circulated underground, and not legally published in Poland until 2000.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: The Katyn Wood Murders
entries.description_ban: All of Mackiewicz's work was banned in Poland 1945–1990; the Katyń massacre was an absolute censorship taboo and his eyewitness account was prohibited.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL ban; documented via Wikipedia.

entries.title: The Eighth Day of the Week
entries.description_ban: Banned in Poland for portraying postwar 'grey misery' too vividly; smuggled abroad and published in Paris (1958).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL ban; documented by the University of Kansas guide.
entries.source_name: University of Kansas — Banned Books in Communist Poland

entries.title: Kompleks polski
entries.description_ban: Censorship intervention led Konwicki to publish 'The Polish Complex' independently via the underground press. Distinct from his A Minor Apocalypse already held.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific PRL suppression; documented by the University of Kansas guide.
entries.source_name: University of Kansas — Banned Books in Communist Poland

entries.title: Siedem dalekich rejsów
entries.description_ban: The PRL censorship office GUKPPiW denied publication, stating the book 'vilified the socio-political situation in Poland'; it appeared abroad.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific GUKPPiW ban; documented by the University of Kansas guide.
entries.source_name: University of Kansas — Banned Books in Communist Poland

entries.title: Animal Farm
entries.description_ban: Possession of Orwell's anti-totalitarian satire was a punishable offence in communist Poland; it circulated widely in samizdat and became a Solidarity-era touchstone. Adds a Poland ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Poland ban; documented by Book Analysis.

entries.description_ban: Banned under the PRL regime and circulated in samizdat; even Orwell-themed ephemera was treated as a serious offence. Adds a Poland ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Poland ban; documented by Book Analysis.

entries.title: The Gulag Archipelago
entries.description_ban: Solzhenitsyn's exposé of the Soviet camp system was suppressed across the Soviet bloc, including the PRL, and circulated in samizdat until 1989. Adds a Poland ban to the existing record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a Poland ban; documented via Wikipedia.
